1: Revelation of God. A false balance is an abomination to the LORD, but a just weight is his delight — the divine character revealed in commercial justice. The LORD who hates deceptive weights is the God of exact measure. In Christ, God's measure is fully revealed: the cross is the exact weight placed on the scales of justice — neither more nor less than what the debt of sin required. Christ himself is the just weight by which all things are measured (Revelation 19:11 — in righteousness he judges). Every human economic injustice points toward the need for the one in whom God's exact justice is satisfied.
2: Theme. When pride comes, then comes disgrace, but with the humble is wisdom — the pride-disgrace / humility-wisdom connection. The Philippians hymn (Philippians 2:5-11) is the NT's most concentrated statement of this principle at the divine scale: the Son who did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped humbled himself to the point of death, and was therefore highly exalted. Christ's humiliation is both the demonstration of perfect wisdom and the ground for his exaltation — the proverb's pattern embodied maximally.
3: Theme. The integrity of the upright guides them, but the crookedness of the treacherous destroys them — integrity as the navigator. Christ's prayer for his disciples' sanctification is that they be kept in God's name and in his truth (John 17:11, 17) — the truth that functions as the guide of the upright. The crookedness of the treacherous that destroys them is the internal inconsistency of a divided allegiance — the double-minded man of James 1:8 — and ultimately, the self-destruction of Judas, whose treachery (begidim, the treacherous) was precisely his crookedness.
4: Theme. Riches do not profit in the day of wrath, but righteousness delivers from death — the wrath-day and righteousness as deliverance. Paul's argument in Romans 5:9: we have been justified by his blood, much more shall we be saved by him from the wrath of God. The righteousness that delivers from death on the day of wrath is Christ's righteousness credited to those who trust him. No wealth, status, or achievement survives the day of wrath — only the alien righteousness of the One who bore the wrath in the sinner's place delivers from what Proverbs identifies as the ultimate threat.
5: Type. The righteousness of the blameless keeps his way straight, but the wicked falls by his own wickedness — straight-way through righteousness. Christ is the perfectly blameless one (tamim, whole, complete) whose way was kept straight through his entire life and through his death. The sin that trips the wicked and makes them fall by their own wickedness is the sin he bore without himself falling. The straight way he walked is the path now open to those in him — not because they walk it perfectly but because he walked it perfectly on their behalf.
6: Theme. The righteousness of the upright delivers them, but the treacherous are taken captive by their lust — delivered righteousness vs. captive lust. The slavery-to-sin diagnosis of Romans 6:16-18 develops the captive-by-lust observation: you are slaves to the one you obey. Christ's liberation is the freedom from this captivity — if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed (John 8:36). The delivery from captivity is not by willpower but by the righteousness that belongs to the upright through union with the one who embodies upright perfectly.
7: Theme. When the wicked dies, his hope perishes, and the expectation of wealth perishes too — the death-ends-all reality for those whose hope is horizontal. Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 15:19 describes the alternative: if in Christ we have hope only in this life, we are of all people most to be pitied. The wicked whose hope perishes at death is the one who has staked everything on this-world outcomes. Christ's resurrection is the ground of hope that does not perish at death — the counterpoint to the proverb's observation is the empty tomb.
8: Theme. The righteous is delivered from trouble, and the wicked walks into it instead — the substitution pattern: trouble that was coming for the righteous arrives at the wicked. This is the normal providential pattern Proverbs observes; its inversion is the gospel: the trouble (wrath, judgment, death) that was coming for the wicked arrived at the righteous One who voluntarily entered the trouble to absorb it. Christ is the righteous one who walked into the trouble meant for others — the substitutionary logic the proverb observes at the social level becomes the atonement at the cosmic level.
9: Revelation of God. With his mouth the godless man would destroy his neighbor, but by knowledge the righteous are delivered — destructive speech vs. knowledge-bearing speech as deliverance. The godless man whose mouth destroys is the false teacher who ruins those who trust him — the NT expands this to include false prophets (Matthew 7:15; 2 Peter 2:1). The knowledge that delivers the righteous is ultimately the knowledge of Christ: eternal life is knowing you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent (John 17:3). The true knowledge is the counterpower to the destructive word.
10: Theme. When it goes well with the righteous, the city rejoices, and when the wicked perish there are shouts of gladness — the community-affect of righteousness and wickedness. The city that rejoices at the righteous man's well-being anticipates the new Jerusalem where God himself will be with them as their God (Revelation 21:3) and where joy is the defining atmosphere. The rejoicing at the wicked's perishing is the counterpart — the judgment that removes the oppressor is itself an occasion for communal joy (Revelation 19:1-4, the heavenly rejoicing at Babylon's fall).
11: Theme. By the blessing of the upright a city is exalted, but by the mouth of the wicked it is overthrown — the blessing of the upright as civic architecture. Abraham's intercession for Sodom (Genesis 18:22-33) is the paradigmatic example: the upright man's blessing was the potential salvation of the city. Christ's intercession for the city — weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) and praying for his executioners (Luke 23:34) — is the maximum expression of the upright man whose blessing maintains the city. The mouth of the wicked that overthrows is the antithesis: the false prophets and corrupt leaders who dismantled Jerusalem.
12: Theme. Whoever belittles his neighbor lacks sense, but a man of understanding remains silent — contempt vs. wise silence. The wise silence of understanding is the character of the one who does not use words to diminish others. Christ's silence before his accusers (Matthew 26:63; Proverbs 27:12-14) is the most striking embodiment of wise restraint — the man of understanding who remains silent when slander is poured out on him. His silence fulfills Isaiah 53:7: he opened not his mouth — the sheep before its shearers is dumb.
13: Theme. Whoever goes about slandering reveals secrets, but he who is trustworthy in spirit keeps a thing covered — faithful discretion as a relational virtue. Christ's instruction about the treatment of a brother's sin (Matthew 18:15-17) follows this principle: the private confrontation before any public disclosure reflects the trustworthy spirit that covers rather than exposes. John's Gospel consistently presents Christ as the one who knows all things about people (John 2:24-25; Proverbs 4:17-18) but exercises that knowledge with perfect discretion — revealing only what serves the person's redemption.
14: Theme. Where there is no guidance, a people falls, but in an abundance of counselors there is safety — counsel-plurality as a safeguard. The Spirit is given as Counselor (Paraclete, John 14:16, 26; Proverbs 15:26; Proverbs 16:7) — Christ explicitly provides the abundance of divine counsel through his Spirit's presence in the church. The Christ-formed community that takes decisions in the Spirit under the wisdom of multiple voices embodies what Proverbs identifies as the safety-producing condition. The council of advisors becomes the Spirit-filled assembly.
15: Theme. Whoever puts up security for a stranger will surely suffer harm, but he who hates striking hands in pledge is secure — the financial-guaranty warning. The antitype: Christ stood surety for those who were his enemies — he who knew no sin was made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21). He is the guarantor (engyos, surety) of a better covenant (Hebrews 7:22). The wisdom tradition warns against reckless surety-pledge; the gospel announces that God in Christ took the ultimate surety — suffering the harm Proverbs warns against, on behalf of the strangers.
16: Theme. A gracious woman gets honor, and violent men get riches — the gendered observation about honor-routes. The gracious woman of Proverbs is the wisdom-embodiment that the book consistently develops toward the eshet chayil of chapter 31. At the level of type, the gracious woman who receives honor is the type of the church — the bride of Christ (Revelation 19:7-8; Ephesians 5:25-27) who is clothed with righteousness and receives the honor of union with the Lamb. Her honor comes through grace rather than violent grasping.
17: Revelation of God. A man who is kind benefits himself, but a cruel man hurts himself — the self-directed benefit of kindness. The divine kindness (chesed) that characterizes God is the model for human kindness — be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you (Ephesians 4:32). Christ's kindness is simultaneously his own beatitude and the pattern for those united to him. The proverb's self-benefit is the paradox of the kingdom: the one who gives himself for others receives himself back; the cruel one who grasps loses even what he has.
18: Theme. The wicked earns deceptive wages, but one who sows righteousness gets a sure reward — sowing righteousness for a sure reward. The harvest/sowing metaphor runs throughout NT ethics: whoever sows to the Spirit will from the Spirit reap eternal life (Galatians 6:8). Christ is himself both the sower (Matthew 13:37) and the seed sown (John 12:24) — the grain of wheat that falls into the earth and produces the harvest of many. The sure reward of righteousness-sowing is grounded in his resurrection as the firstfruits (1 Corinthians 15:20).
19: Theme. Whoever is steadfast in righteousness will live, but he who pursues evil will die — the righteousness-life / evil-death equation. Romans 8:6 states the same principle in pneumatological terms: to set the mind on the flesh is death, but to set the mind on the Spirit is life and peace. The steadfast righteousness that produces life is not achieved through willpower but received through the Spirit — the life-producing direction of heart orientation being the same in both texts.
20: Revelation of God. Those of crooked heart are an abomination to the LORD, but those of blameless ways are his delight — the divine delight in blamelessness. The Father's declaration over Christ — this is my beloved Son in whom I am well pleased (Matthew 3:17; Proverbs 17:5) — is the maximum expression of this divine delight. Those united to Christ participate in this delight: he has made us accepted in the Beloved (Ephesians 1:6). The blameless-ways-as-delight is the orientation of the justified community — their ways are not their own blamelessness but their participation in the Blameless One.
21: Theme. Be assured, an evil person will not go unpunished, but the offspring of the righteous will be delivered — the sure punishment / sure deliverance. The certainty formula (yad leyad, hand to hand — a sealing gesture affirming the certainty) introduces both sides. The deliverance of the righteous offspring anticipates the resurrection community — those who bear the character of the righteous one. Christ's resurrection is the first instance of the offspring-of-righteousness being delivered; his people share this deliverance as co-heirs (Romans 8:17).
22: Theme. Like a gold ring in a pig's snout is a beautiful woman without discretion — the incoherence between outward beauty and inner character. The reverse applies to the church as Christ's bride: not outwardly splendid but inwardly adorned — the unfading beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit (1 Peter 3:4). Paul's description of the church Christ presents to himself without spot or wrinkle (Ephesians 5:27) is the gold ring on the finger that belongs to it — discretion (inner character) and beauty (outward glory) in genuine correspondence, which only Christ can accomplish in his bride.
23: Theme. The desire of the righteous ends only in good, the expectation of the wicked in wrath — the endpoint-of-desire formula. The beatitude: blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied (Matthew 5:6). The desire of the righteous that ends in good is the desire shaped by the kingdom's values — which Christ promises will be satisfied. The wrath that waits for the wicked's expectation is the day when all that horizontal hope meets the divine assessment.
24: Theme. One gives freely, yet grows all the richer; another withholds what he should give, and only suffers want — the paradox of generous increase. John 12:24-25: unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit. Whoever loves his life loses it, and whoever hates his life in this world will keep it for eternal life. The freely-giving-yet-growing-richer is the grain-of-wheat principle — the logic of the cross and resurrection. Christ is the first and foundational instance of the proverb's paradox.
25: Theme. Whoever brings blessing will be enriched, and one who waters will himself be watered — the reciprocal watering principle. The fountain-of-water that becomes a spring within the one who gives is the Johannine promise: the water I give will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life (John 4:14). Those who give freely from what Christ gives do not deplete but increase — the water flowing through them from Christ replenishes what they pour out. The blesser-enriched principle is grounded in the inexhaustible source from which the blessing flows.
26: Theme. The people curse him who holds back grain, but a blessing is on the head of him who sells it — the grain-sharing and community blessing. The feeding of the five thousand is the most direct enactment of the grain-sharing principle: Christ takes what is withheld by scarcity and distributes it freely, drawing the blessing of the crowd. More fundamentally, Christ is the Bread of Life (John 6:35) — the withheld grain of the world's hunger, freely distributed, is the figure of the one in whom all spiritual hunger is satisfied.
27: Revelation of God. Whoever diligently seeks good seeks favor, but evil comes to him who searches for it — seeking as the determinant of what one finds. Christ's promise: ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find (Matthew 7:7). The diligent seeking of good that finds divine favor is the seeking of the kingdom — which is precisely what Christ instructs his disciples to make primary. Those who search for evil find it not because God sends it but because they orient themselves toward it and thus navigate their way into it.
28: Theme. Whoever trusts in his riches will fall, but the righteous will flourish like a green leaf — the wealth-trust collapse vs. the flourishing of the righteous. Christ's Sermon on the Mount addresses the wealth-trust problem directly: you cannot serve God and money (Matthew 6:24). The green-leaf flourishing of the righteous is the vine-branch fruitfulness of John 15 — those attached to the true vine bear fruit continuously, their flourishing sourced not in stored resources but in continuous dependence on the life flowing from the vine.
29: Theme. Whoever troubles his own household will inherit the wind, and the fool will be servant to the wise of heart — the household-trouble self-curse. Ephesians 5-6 develops the household-management theology of the NT, grounding it in the Christ-church relationship: those who trouble their households by self-centered authority rather than sacrificial love destroy the very structure they inhabit. The wind-inheritance is the eschatological emptiness of the one who consumed his household rather than nourishing it.
30: Type / Theme. The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, and whoever captures souls is wise — the tree of life and soul-winning. The tree of life is the eschatological image from Eden (Genesis 2:9) restored in Revelation (Revelation 22:2) — and Christ is the one through whom access to the tree of life is regained: to the one who conquers I will grant to eat of the tree of life (Revelation 2:7). The fruit of the perfectly righteous one is the restoration of the tree of life to those who were barred from it. The laqach nefashot (capturing souls) is the image of the apostolic mission: I will make you fishers of men (Matthew 4:19).
31: Theme. If the righteous is repaid on earth, how much more the wicked and the sinner! — the a fortiori argument from the righteous man's earthly experiences of suffering to the certain judgment of the wicked. Peter quotes this verse directly (1 Peter 4:17-18): for it is time for judgment to begin at the household of God; and if it begins with us, what will be the outcome for those who do not obey the gospel of God? The a fortiori logic from Christ's own suffering (the righteous repaid on earth — his cross) to the certain judgment of the unrepentant is precisely the argument Peter inherits from the proverb.
